title: Share of children achieving minimum reading proficiency by the end of lower-secondary age
caption:
Share of children achieving minimum reading proficiency by the end of lower-secondary age, 2022

Percentage of children at lower secondary school age meeting reading comprehension standards. This data accounts for school completion rates to estimate reading skills for all children, not just those in school.

Across nations in 2022, the share of children reaching minimum reading proficiency by the end of lower-secondary age spans a wide range, from near-universal coverage in some high-income countries to very low levels in others. The vertical axis measures the percentage of children meeting reading comprehension standards, ranging from 0% to over 80%. Bars are colored by income group: low-income (dark purple), lower-middle-income (light purple), upper-middle-income (light green), and high-income (dark green). Ireland leads with a share above 80%, followed closely by Australia, Lithuania, and Spain, all in the high-income group and all above 70%. Turkey and Greece sit near the middle of the distribution, with shares around 65–70%, while Moldova and Brazil fall in the 40–50% band. Kazakhstan and Azerbaijan appear in the upper-middle-income group with shares between 30% and 40%. At the lower end, the Dominican Republic and Cambodia show shares below 20%, with Cambodia’s bar falling under 10%. High-income countries dominate the top of the chart, while lower-middle-income countries cluster toward the bottom. Upper-middle-income countries occupy the middle and lower-middle portions, with some overlap into higher shares. The pattern shows a clear gradient: higher national income groups tend to have higher shares of children achieving minimum reading proficiency, with the lowest shares concentrated among lower-middle-income countries.
